label: 231_historical echoes_echoes_post author_research librarian
description: Amy Farber explores how historical references and humor are used in economic communications. From financial ads to literary allusions, these narratives reflect on the enduring themes of economics through time.

earliest_articles.title: Time and work at the Bank of England
earliest_articles.excerpt: In 1783 the Bank of England appointed a Committee of Inspection to examine working practices within its departments and identify any failings in procedures. The committee spent a year interviewing the clerks and observing them at work. Their final report offers a comprehensive record of the bank’s operations at a time…

earliest_articles.title: Historical Echoes: More than Dead Presidents
earliest_articles.excerpt: New York Fed Research LibraryFrogs? Santa Claus? Goddesses? In the past, U.S. currency has featured much more than just the images of deceased presidents and pyramids. For a view of some surprising images that have appeared on U.S. paper currency, see “Symbols on American Money,” published by the Federal Reserve Bank…

earliest_articles.title: Historical Echoes: Lights, Camera, Action! The Board’s New Headquarters
earliest_articles.excerpt: New York Fed Research LibraryDuring the 1930s, major events were often announced by newsreels shown in movie theaters before feature films. This is how the Fed’s new headquarters made itsfilm debutin 1937.Construction began in 1935, twenty-two years after the creation of the Federal Reserve System and following the…

newest_articles.excerpt: An Empirical History of the United States Postal Savings System Working Paper 25812 DOI 10.3386/w25812 Issue Date Seeking to reach the unbanked, the United States Postal Savings System provided a federally insured savings alternative to traditional banks. newest_articles.title: Historical Echoes: The Bank That Never Existed, or You Say ‘Immigrant’ and I Say ‘Emigrant’
newest_articles.excerpt: Amy FarberJust because an entity is referred to all over the place, does that mean it exists? We are not talking about heaven or hell. We are talking about the “Immigrant Savings Bank.”Let’s backtrack for a second. Suppose you’re walking along the street in New York and you see a sign that says “Emigrant Savings…

newest_articles.title: Historical Echoes: That Pesky, Well‑Overdue Library Book
newest_articles.excerpt: Amy FarberThe “extremely overdue library book” has had a long run as a sitcom trope. central_articles.title: Historical Echoes: Echoes, Schmechoes, This Post Only Has a Drop of History in It
central_articles.excerpt: Amy FarberYou might hear: “Economy eschmonomy.” Another possibility is: “Economy schmeconomy.” This phenomenon of repeating a word with the prefix shm- (or sometimes “schm-”), is called shm-reduplication. It challenges the relevance and sometimes the value of the repeated word, and examples can be found in articles…
